question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user craft two distinct transaction query URLs (e.g., /api/v1/transactions/0.0.10-123 vs /api/v1/transactions/0.0.10-123?) that produce the same MD5 hash via crypto.createHash('md5').update(req.originalUrl).digest('hex'), causing a cache key collision that serves one user's transaction status to another, leading to false transaction confirmation or denial?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the known MD5 collision vulnerability in crypto.createHash('md5') to generate two different transaction endpoint URLs with identical digests, poisoning the cache entry for a legitimate transaction lookup with attacker-controlled transaction data, causing victims to receive falsified transaction results?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user abuse the lack of URL normalization (noted in the comment 'In the future, this will utilize Edwin's request normalizer (9113)') in cacheKeyGenerator() by appending redundant query parameters (e.g., ?order=asc&order=asc) to a transaction URL, bypassing the cache and forcing a fresh DB read that reflects a tampered or replayed transaction state?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of query parameter normalization in cacheKeyGenerator() by reordering query parameters (e.g., ?limit=10&order=asc vs ?order=asc&limit=10) on a transaction endpoint to generate different cache keys for semantically identical requests, causing cache fragmentation that prevents legitimate users from receiving cached transaction data and forces repeated DB queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user use URL-encoded variants of a transaction ID (e.g., %30.%30.%31%30 vs 0.0.10) in req.originalUrl to generate a different MD5 cache key for the same transaction, bypassing a cached response that shows a transaction as failed and forcing a fresh lookup that may show a different status, enabling double-spend confusion?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a crafted if-none-match header matching a known ETag value of a cached transaction response to receive a 304 UNMODIFIED status code, falsely confirming that a submitted transaction's state is unchanged when in fact the underlying transaction has been reversed or failed, leading to incorrect finality assumptions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user brute-force or enumerate ETag values (stored in cachedResponse.headers[ETAG_HEADER]) for transaction endpoints by sending repeated if-none-match requests, confirming the existence and cached state of specific submitted transactions without triggering a full response, leaking transaction metadata?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Object.assign(new CachedApiResponse(), redisValue) deserialization pattern to trigger prototype pollution if a Redis entry (reachable via cache key prediction) contains __proto__ or constructor keys, corrupting the CachedApiResponse prototype and causing all subsequent transaction responses to return attacker-controlled data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheCheckHandler() serves cached transaction data without re-validating against the database or blockchain, causing a victim querying a transaction endpoint to receive a stale cached response showing a transaction as successful when it has since been determined invalid, enabling fraudulent confirmation of submitted transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user race against the cache population window (between responseCacheCheckHandler() returning a miss and responseCacheUpdateHandler() writing the entry) to submit a transaction and then immediately query its status, receiving a stale or empty cache response that misrepresents the transaction's consensus state?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a HEAD request to a transaction endpoint to probe whether a specific transaction is cached (via the isHead branch that calls negotiate() and res.end() without body), leaking information about which transactions have been recently queried and are in the cache, without triggering full response logging?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the direct use of redisTtl in the cache-control header (`public, max-age=${redisTtl}`) without sanitization, where a Redis entry with a manipulated TTL value (e.g., negative or zero) causes downstream proxies or clients to treat a transaction response as immediately expired or permanently cached, disrupting transaction status visibility?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the logger.info() call that logs req.originalUrl unsanitized (`${req.ip} ${req.method} ${req.originalUrl} from cache`) by injecting newline characters or log-format tokens into a transaction URL, causing log injection that obscures audit trails of tampered transaction queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a cache deception attack by requesting a transaction URL with a crafted path suffix (e.g., /api/v1/transactions/0.0.10-123/static.css) that causes the cache middleware to store a transaction response under a key that a victim's browser or proxy will later request as a static asset, exposing transaction data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CACHE_CONTROL_REGEX = /^.*max-age=(\\d+)/ pattern, which uses a greedy .* prefix, by crafting a cache-control header value like 'no-store, max-age=99999' that matches the regex and causes responseCacheUpdateHandler() to store a transaction response with an arbitrarily large TTL, keeping stale transaction data in cache for an extended period?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault() falling back to DEFAULT_REDIS_EXPIRY = 1 when no cache-control header is present, causing a transaction response to be cached for only 1 second, and then race within that window to query the same transaction endpoint and receive a stale response before the cache expires?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the parseInt(maxAge[1], 10) call in getCacheControlExpiryOrDefault() by causing a cache-control header to contain a very large max-age value (e.g., max-age=2147483647), causing responseCacheUpdateHandler() to store a transaction response in Redis with an enormous TTL, effectively freezing the transaction's cached state permanently?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the spread of res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] into the headers object (`...(res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] ?? {})`) without sanitization, injecting attacker-controlled headers into the cached transaction response that will be served to all subsequent users querying the same transaction endpoint?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the isUnmodified branch that stores httpStatusCodes.OK.code (200) in cache when the actual response was 304, causing a transaction endpoint to always return 200 from cache even when the transaction data has not changed, masking the true modification state of submitted transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a cache stampede by sending a large number of concurrent requests to the same transaction endpoint during a cache miss window, causing all requests to bypass responseCacheCheckHandler() simultaneously and flood the database with identical transaction queries, resulting in denial of service for transaction status lookups?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user send a malformed accept-encoding header (e.g., 'gzip;q=0, *;q=0') to the negotiate() function via new Negotiator(req), causing the content-encoding and content-length headers to be set incorrectly for a cached transaction response, leading to response body truncation or corruption that misrepresents the transaction data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the inaccurate content-length calculation in getLength() (`Math.floor((this.body.length * 3) / 4)`) for compressed responses, by requesting a gzip-encoded transaction response where the approximated length differs from the actual compressed length, causing HTTP response desynchronization that corrupts the transaction data received by the client?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user send an accept-encoding header of 'gzip' to negotiate() to receive a compressed transaction response, then compare the content-length header (set via cachedResponse.getLength()) against the actual body length to detect whether the cached transaction body has been tampered with or truncated in Redis?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that negotiate() always sets the Vary header to 'accept-encoding' (res.setHeader(VARY_HEADER, 'accept-encoding')), causing intermediate proxies to cache separate copies of a transaction response per encoding, and then poison one encoding variant's cache entry to serve falsified transaction data to clients requesting that encoding?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: shouldCompress()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shouldCompress() threshold check (`body.length >= config.cache.response.compressThreshold`) by crafting a transaction query that returns a response body exactly at the compression threshold boundary, causing inconsistent compression behavior across cache hits and misses that leads to content-length mismatches and response parsing errors?
